name: senior_driver_ai_renewal_screen_mobility_bias_claim
premise: DMV director Casey Walker must decide whether to mandate an AI simulator retest for drivers seventy-five and older after internal data shows reaction-time bias against arthritis medication users. Disability advocate Sage Jones argues the screen correlates with zip-code clinic access, not competence. Suspending the screen returns to long human queues that strand rural seniors without licenses for groceries. Keeping it may violate ADA pattern claims. External scrutiny from regulators and community observers has intensified because the outcome could redefine expectations in Transportation Policy. The dilemma is widely framed as a liberty vs equality tradeoff under incomplete information and tightening deadlines that discourage indefinite delay.

agents.name: Casey Walker
agents.identity: I am the DMV director accountable for highway safety trends, ADA compliance, and equitable access to mobility in car-dependent counties.
agents.goal: I want crash risk reduced without a de facto mobility ban on seniors who cannot reach simulator labs.

agents.name: Sage Jones
agents.identity: I am a disability rights attorney preparing a class pattern claim if the AI screen stays mandatory without individualized accommodations.
agents.goal: I want the screen suspended until medical review officers can override false fails same day, even if queues explode.

choices: I will mandate the AI screen only in counties with mobile simulator vans deployed within fourteen days, accepting uneven safety policy geography temporarily.
I will suspend the AI screen statewide and hire retired examiners surge panels with overtime bonds, accepting budget overruns and longer wait times for all ages.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Governor's press conference is in two hours. Casey Walker must choose between county-conditional AI screening with mobile vans or statewide suspension with human surge panels. What does Casey Walker do?
